It seems wrong to disable the 'bind by default' behavior without at least some consideration for why that call was there. I didn't write the code from which that call originated, so I'm unsure what the motivations were at the time.

I did some searches, and I'm not exactly sure what the motivation is for allowing a client to customize the bind socket. I found [this SO question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4118241/what-client-side-situations-need-bind) which has [only one useful answer](http://stackoverflow.com/a/4118325/70170). If that answer is right, and the _only_ reason one would use bind is to specify the port, but it seems entirely plausible to me that there are other reasons. [This post explains one](https://idea.popcount.org/2014-04-03-bind-before-connect/) and I surmise that binding to a specific address could also prove useful when wanting to select a particular interface.

That said, it seems to me that 'bind before connect' is an unusual paradigm that's rarely used, so probably shouldn't be the default behavior for a lib like this one.

> Execution of something like `sock.bind (('127.11.210.3', 0))` will give a permission denied error.

In your example, you provide a specific bind address, but your patch suggests that also execution of `sock.bind(('', 0))` will give the same permission error. Is that the case?

Really this is a bug in `selectors` -- I guess it's possible there might still be time to fix it upstream before 3.6, though that doesn't help in the short term. I wonder how asyncio avoids it -- maybe they unconditionally create the waker socket, so they never ran into it?

If folks want to improve windows support, then some concrete things that might be worth considering:
* Make a PR to work around the issue in curio. There are a few possible strategies that might work -- maybe just unconditionally calling `_init_loopback` at kernel startup would be enough. Or @mafemergency's clever hack. Or even monkeypatching `SelectSelector._select` to do the right thing...
* submit a patch to Python upstream to avoid this issue. I guess `SelectSelector` on windows should check for all-empty before calling `select.select` and fall back to just doing a `time.sleep` otherwise. Or maybe `select.select` should implement the workaround directly.
* Set up Appveyor testing for curio, so that new changes get tested on windows

I'm back in tkinter land after an excursion to qt...

Just re-read this thread and the pieces are coming together.

I'm wanting to have a curio task managing the events from tk/qt/whatever.

I think the files involved can be found near here:

https://github.com/tcltk/tcl/blob/master/generic/tclMain.c#L662

Plus tkinter.Tk,foo() talks directly for a lot of foo's.

For now, I have a polling Tk loop that (will) do two things:

a. pass on events to the gui toolkit so it does its thing ie manage the screen

b. listen to input events:  keyboard, mouse, whatever..  and turn them into a common language.  That gets put into curio.UniversalQueue's

If we can avoid the polling then the UI performance should go way up.

From there lots of fascinating options.

Current goal: tickle swarms of glittering stinging bats

I agree that C++ would probably make some of this more straightforward. It would probably be a good move if one were to write SEP from scratch. However, there are a few reasons to stick with plain C, such as coherency of the codebase as a whole (most of which is legacy C-style), and simpler interop of the C library with other languages (e.g., Julia).

I arrived at a solution that uses a multi-line buffer of floats. The buffer manages converting lines from the image as needed. `convolve` and `matched_filter` are now just single functions that operate on these buffers. Seems to work well and it was a net decrease in lines of code. If you're interested, the code is in the `conv-refactor` branch and some relevant areas are:

- https://github.com/kbarbary/sep/blob/conv-refactor/src/extract.h#L61 `arraybuffer` struct.
- https://github.com/kbarbary/sep/blob/conv-refactor/src/extract.c#L60 `arraybuffer`-related functions.

The branch already passes the tests, but I'd like to see if I can clean up the meaning of the `cdscan`/`cdwscan` buffers a bit more before merging.

Regarding masks, yes there are work-arounds using the error array, but it would be nice to take a third array as a separate input, so that you don't have to edit the error array. Should be pretty easy to add using the `arraybuffer` interface.

This looks like a powerful format to combine bunches of settings based on various modes, compilers etc... congrats ! 

It does not save the graph of dependencies though. The user has to rely on FoBiS internals (and trust back-compatibility) to be sure the exact same dependencies will be analyzed in future releases of FoBiS (who knows which version will be installed on the user's computer who has my code). That is where a basic Makefile reassures everyone (I definitely want to have a Makefile git-tracked in my project !)

One minor, low-priority suggestion for the fobos format:

- __`directories=` parameter__ : in addition to `src` and `exclude`, have an optional `directories` (with similar functionality as python distutils' `packages`, but as : "dir1", "dir1/dir2", etc...) : increase user control AND clarity of what the program will be doing simply by looking at the fobos file. This is a tradeoff (IMO, fair) between full-automation and possible surprises (current FoBiS, despite all the good) and precise (over?)-determination (Makefile). I guess I am used to (and trust) python.

And in general, something easy to keep track of things: give FoBiS.py the option to generate the _fobos_ file as well as the generated _dependency graph_ when run by hand, and to re-use them! The first would help writing templates / more complete fobos, the second would increase user control as well as compile time. Both would help ascertain what FoBiS.py does. 

But again, from all these suggestions (since all they are concerned with is to increase reliability of FoBiS.py), have a correct Makefile that closely reflects FoBiS.py behaviour sounds the most important step... Ok, I stop with that ;-)

To temper my push toward Makefiles: your whole project is really impressive, so thanks and keep going !

Hi Ilya,
you can test the ldap3 library without any ldap server, I've set a few LDAP
cloud servers. Just uncomment line 66 in ldap3/test/__int__.py:

```
location = 'TRAVIS,SYNC,0,EDIR'  # forces configuration as if we're running on Travis
```
you should also set test_fast_decoder to False at line 47 to use pyans1 for
decoding returning LDAP messages too.

To run the test please use
```
python -m unittest
```
at the root level of the ldap3 repository. This will run the tests against an eDirectory LDAP Server.


Following RFCs OctetString should always be utf-8 in LDAP. I know there are
some exception to this (notably Active Directory) but with 0.2.2 I didn't
have any error.

Anyway I will checkj the code today. Probably getting the bytes() value and
then performing the encoding could be a good solution.

Bye,
Giovanni

From what I call tell, results of the iterations of optimization are written to database via the save method. The arguments to that call are (objectivefunction,parameterlist,simulations,chains):
like is either a float or a list of floats if multi-objective-function
param is a list of param values or a list thereof if multiple chains
simulation can be anything (normally 1d-array) or a list thereof if multiple chains
chain is an integer or a list of chain indices if multiple chains

If ram is defined, indivual lists are built using append, results are thus stored in memory.
If csv is definded, results are dynamically written to file, and not stored in memory.

I think that saving nd-arrays calls for another method (writing to csv takes too much time, and storing the results in memory may be also very expensive). Probably implementing a custom method would make sense (that is not merged to spotpy). Still, I'll try to figure out a way to outsource all the manipulations on the (objectivefunction,parameterlist,simulations,chains) in the algorithms to the database method.

```

def scaleDrawing(drawing,factor,showBoundary=False):
    """
    scale a reportlab.graphics.shapes.Drawing() object, 
    leaving its aspect ratio unchanged
    """
    sx=sy=factor
    drawing.width,drawing.height = drawing.minWidth()*sx, drawing.height*sy
    drawing.scale(sx,sy)
    if showBoundary:
        drawing._showBoundary = True
    
    return drawing

```

Ok, here is my take on speeding things up. 

----


Some performance enhancement.

- Make sure the `CachedFastParser` are cached on disk
- Change the pickle serialisation on disk is optimized **on write**,
this does not change the pickling version, it just make it more
efficient (unless things have already been written)
- Do not trust the `SequenceMatcher` to be efficient if the two files
are the same, just take a shortcut and return previous completer.
- Do some argument caching in the core of the parser loop.

With this the 2 remaining method that could be made faster and are
taking most of the time are:
    - the `splitlines` one, but hard to be made much faster
    - the `pickle.load` one, if we come up with our own serialisation.

With this, on my machine I have in some case a ~15 fold increase in
speed. Leading to things like `matplotlib.<tab>` becoming reactive
enough to use.


--- 

I've couple that on IPython to compute completion types  at most for the first `0.4` seconds of the spend on the completion generator. So it hopefully should make things reasonable even on slowish machines. 

I'm not ready to write a custom Parser serializer for now.

@rnixx: I have an asyncio.Task (`self.__run_task = asyncio.Task(self.__run())`) that waits to pop items from the command queue, sends the attached command to the server and feeds all lines til the next OK (or any errors) to the item which is a Future that in the course of the lines gets done. (These items are called "results" due to this, maybe CommandInstance would be more suitable).

Some CommandResult items (the CommandResultIterable) are not only a future but also an asynchronous iterable, but that's something __run doesn't directly need to concern itself with.

Furthermore (and that's why it is almost 50 LoC), `__run` also schedules an `idle` on the currently relevant subsystems (so multiple idle with different interests can run concurrently from the library user's point of view) and interrupts with `noidle` when a command comes along or the idle interests change.

You must be running this on a Windows console. The problem is the Windows console font does not support unicode. There are a couple of things you can do.

The simplest is the following.
```
    try:
        # python 3
        sys.stdout = codecs.getwriter('utf8')(sys.stdout.buffer)
    except:
        # python 2
        sys.stdout = codecs.getwriter('utf8')(sys.stdout)
```
With this code you will get square boxes substituted for unicode characters, but at least your code won't crash.

Look at [examples\cli.py](https://github.com/geduldig/TwitterAPI/blob/master/examples/cli.py) for a better solution. Basically, add the following to your code.
```
    if sys.platform == "win32":
        from Unicode_win32 import stdout
        sys.stdout = stdout
```
This also prints out square boxes, but some common unicode characters, like accents, umlauts, etc., get printed correctly.

Neither solution will print out emoji and other similar unicode characters -- unless you are on a Mac or some other type of console that supports unicode.

another thing with this setup borrowed from ttfautohint-py, is that the source distribution as generated with `python setupt.py sdist` will not include the freetype2 source. So, for example, running `tox` won't work, because tox by default creates the sdist, then installs it into the virtual environment, before running the tests inside it (ok, one can also do `tox --develop` or set `usedevelop=True` in `tox.ini`). 

In any case, building the embedded library currently requires access to the freetype2 git submodule (thus one has to `git clone --recursive ...` or do `git submodule update --init` afterwards). And this submodule is not included in the python package sdist. I don't think it would be a good idea trying to add all the freetype2 sources from the git submodule to the python sdist package, because freetype's own build system probably detects whether it's running from a git repository or a released tarball.

One option instead of using git submodules would be to have the setup.py itself download the freetype2 released tarball from the official website, and do the build from there.
Using the released tarballs also has the advantage that you won't need to do `./autogen.sh` but can do `./configure` directly, so you don't need all the autotools but just make.

Actually, the only reason I used git submodules for ttfautohint-py was that at the time I wrote it, the stable ttfautohint release didn't have an option to build a shared library. If I were to redo it now, I would rather use the stable releases instead of the git repositories.

Hi Daniel,

thanks for your scrutiny. I believe the code is correct, when used as intended, but also unclear
(or counter-intuitive).  

Line 71:

    f = sci.interp1d(wlprime, flux, bounds_error=False, fill_value=fv)

essentially gives the shifted spectrum and line 72:

    nflux = f(wvl)

the shifted spectrum on the original wavelength axis.

If you start with wvl and flux as your starting values and obtain

    nflux, wlprime = dopplerShift(...)

from the doppler shift, then the shifted spectrum is given by
(wvl, nflux) and/or (wlprime, flux). If you use (wlprime, nflux), you get a double shift, indeed.

When writing this, I thought it was a good idea, because I mostly need the shifted spectrum
at the input wavelengths, but sometime one would need the updated wavelength axis.
However, realizing this "cross-over" between input and output, it
may not have been such a good idea.

Cheers,

- another exception is raised: the generator has terminated with an error.

As indicated in my original post I don't think it is possible to differentiate between a modify/move and a delete/create with recycled inodes.  So the choice is a lesser of two evils:  Modify/move being identified as delete/create or delete/creates being identified as moves.

What I am suggesting is that modify/move being reported as delete/create is closer to the truth (the original file does no longer exist as it was and there is file in the create path) than real delete/create being reported as a move (this is totally wrong, the files are probably wildly different and totally unrelated).

In our case we simply cannot live with the incorrect move events.  We can live with the modify/move being delete/create as it has the same result as a modify/move (the file is processed in the new location). Yes, I figured this would be possible with PollingObserverVFS but the fork fix was much easier; thanks for the suggestion.  We are sticking with the fork for now as I feel strongly that the code is wrong as-is given inode recycling.  We have stress tested in our load test harness with no issues. We will see if the mysterious outages in production stop once this forked version is deployed.

Hmm, sticky.  Assuming we need the files and form data in one function in order to create a database record or something, I can think of three options:

1) Don't use AJAX for the dropzone component when it is part of a larger form, and lose the nice dropzone validation feedback.  Instead embed the file upload validation feedback in the template like the rest of the form elements.

2) Merge the form data into the AJAX, and allow the user to specify a callback function which get the response (after dropzone has used it) and can update the form elements.

3) Do two requests as you are currently doing and include a common uuid in both requests.  The file handling function can use the key to put the files somewhere where the form data handling function can retrieve them.

#1 clears the dropzone ui so server side per file validation messages can't be shown the way dropzone usually does. (is this a big deal?) .  #2 could be fine but would compel users to figure out how to display field and form validation messages through javascript rather than through the templates like WTForms.  This might even merit a library of it's own, since adding other filed types is probably out of scope for flask-dropzone.  #3 is trivial after the work that has already been done.  It is limited in that form validation cannot influence file validation but I can't really think of a solid example of this being an issue (clearing the dropzone files in the UI on form level validation failure seems fine in all situations I can think of).

I agree - there is a bit of a security issue here and a general maintenance risk, akin to the one that [bit node developers last year](https://www.theregister.co.uk/2016/03/23/npm_left_pad_chaos/).

I've always been concerned about the bus factor and I joined Jazzband early on because I'd like the idea of there being a community around incubating and low-usage libraries. I had planned to move one repository there and see how that went, but looking at the repo list, I don't see any projects I maintain there. I don't recall if I ran into a specific issue or if I just didn't get around to moving a project.

> Would you have wanted Django to add a dependency for lru_cache in 2013-2017?

I think I would have. Instead what you get is another copy of code from a post. This one happened to be stable, but it also appears as if it never worked around the bugs in update_wrapper, which the [published backport did](/jaraco/backports.functools_lru_cache/commit/7f8725325ee8c275e80a28420a962128e0fc6afd). By keeping the backport available only in Django, they leave the non-Django community without a solution. Other projects follow this pattern and you end up with dozens of projects with their own copy of the code, each diverging slightly to suit the needs and whims of that project, and all vulnerable to software erosion as there's no place the backport is reliably maintained across projects.

If someone on the Django project had published the backport, I would have used that rather than publishing it myself.

I find forks and divergent implementations to be one of the most challenging aspects of maintaining mature, enterprise-grade Python projects.

> This could also be the path for `@classproperty`.

I agree it sometimes makes sense to temporarily copy code into a project rather than involve a new dependency. And in fact, for a long time, CherryPy had no dependencies, which made it particularly friendly to fresh Python developers who hadn't yet grasped the nuances of Python Packaging. But the packaging situation has improved dramatically and CherryPy now relies on that infrastructure. But even in the case where the code is copied rather than linked, it's worthwhile to have a canonical source where that code is maintained, so that issues can be filed and resolved and the history can be tracked.

I try to remain humble when it comes to this functionality. In fact, when I identify a project like `more_itertools` that has substantial overlap with projects I've created like `jaraco.itertools`, I seek to [consolidate those efforts](https://github.com/erikrose/more-itertools/issues/32), deferring to the more popular and more broadly-maintained package.

I'd do the same for the `@classproperty` or all of `jaraco.classes` if I stumbled across a relevant implementation.

Still, I think the security and custody and maintenance issues are outside the scope of this ticket.

Ok, so it seems you indeed are encountering the same issue as @etiennethomassen. The issue is with the value you are supplying to the field when you call `record(*values)`. The error says it all: **You are trying to insert a string value (`value is a str: '0'`) into your ID field which is defined as numeric**. Instead of inserting the number 0 you are inserting it as a '0' string. 

To make it more concrete, this does not work:

```
w = shapefile.Writer()
w.field('ID', 'N', '8')
w.record("0")
w.null()
w.save("test.shp")
```

But changing `w.record("0")` to `w.record(0)` works. 

**So your error has nothing to do with how you define the field**. As I said previously, the field width change was only in the docs, **the library accepts it as either a string or a number**. You can write it like this `w.field('ID', 'N', 8)`, or like this `w.field('ID', 'N', '8')`, both works. 

**It worked in 1.2.10, but only because it was a bug**. Passing strings to a numeric field really shouldn't be allowed, a bug that was fixed in 1.2.11. I am will probably end up loosening this criteria a little, only throw an error if the string cannot be forced to a number.

Regarding PEP 526, since you bring it up, I have to respond. I agree that there's no reason why attrs can't support PEP 526; but at least do it right. This is wrong:
```py
y: attr.ib(default=42)
```
This misuses the annotation slot (the thing after `:` and before `=` if there is one). That slot should be used only for a PEP 484 type (or the ClassVar[] notation introduced in PEP 526). IMO the form to support is
```py
y: int = attr.ib(default=42)
```

The default output is PCM stereo audio in a WAV file, which is uncompressed, hence the big file size. The reason for doing so is that the output should be lossless by default.

If you want smaller output files, you have to re-encode the files and compress them using a lossy audio codec such as AAC or MP3. You can do this by setting the `--acodec` option and possibly some extra options like audio bitrate (e.g. 192 kBit/s for AAC which should be transparent):

    ffmpeg-normalize --acodec libfdk_aac --extra-options "-b:a 192k" /path/to/input.mp3

Note that if you want to normalize a bunch of MP3 files without actually modifying the stream or re-compression, you should probably use [MP3Gain](http://mp3gain.sourceforge.net/) instead.

I will either have to use the gemoji source to extract the info I need instead of the github API, or I will need to push such a request to a separate emoji plugin.  With the addition of the aforementioned new emoji which require non-breaking Unicode characters, and the fact there is no way to determine this from the what the Github API returns, it now makes it difficult to automate an update if I am to require the the return of actual Unicode code points.

As gemoji is the source behind Github's emoji, I think it could be used.  I would have to add logic to comb the source and determine the actual code points on update.  I would need to account for Python narrow builds as well, but it is possible.  This would eliminate the ability for the plugin to update on the fly, but I am okay with that.  It would just have to be officially updated on the repo and then built for a release.

What I don't want to do though is to manually manage a list of Unicode code points.  As long as I can come up with an easy automated way to get short names from a common list and extract their Unicode code points, I don't mind doing this.  Whether this would be associated with the githubemoji extension or a separate emoji extension is yet to be determined.

The url suffixes aren't required - they're just a shortcut for `?format=xlsx`.  The `renderer_classes` attribute on the view is what Django REST Framework uses to determine which output formats are supported.  The default renderer_classes on `PandasView` enables all 7 of the renderer classes provided by Django REST Pandas (`csv`, `txt`, `json`, `xlsx`, `xls`, `png`, and `svg`).

All of the content type negotiation is handled in Django REST Framework, so you don't need to hard code anything to switch between formats - it should just work. To determine which output format to generate (i.e., which renderer class to use), DRF looks at the:

1. HTTP `Accepts` header,
2. `?format=` URL keyword, and
3. the format suffix/extension (if configured)

So, when you got this working on OS X it was likely because the default settings already support `?format=xlsx` and not because of the custom `transform_dataframe()` function.  The DRP documentation/README was not clear on this point - I updated it to hopefully clarify a few things.  Thanks again for reporting this.

Sorry for commenting late on this, but I'm **extremely** concerned about
this line of work.

The problem with this is that there is no way to know whether you can
"borrow" the argument memory in a dynamic network since you might decide
later to  access the original value again in the future. You can let the
caller declare "feel free to reuse this!" but this exposes the programmer
to hard to track down bugs, and it makes the toolkit hard to understand and
hard to learn.

I realise memory usage is important, but simplicity is important too l.
Next year, GPUs will have more memory but complex code will still be
complex. DyNet is designed to be simple and fast, not the most resource
efficient. Let TF and the static toolkits handle extreme memory efficiency
(after all, they can do the static analysis that will reveal whether a
value will be needed again in the future).

The problem is that components get into spiral of `create -> start -> die -> destroy`. So if we want to alert on a component dying we can only do that on the `die` state which isn't ideal since the `die` state is used all over the container lifecycle. 

The `die` event could be triggered because of restarts or application upgrades. 

Instead, we should trigger alerts because the container is in the `stopped` state. This means it has exited and all restarts have been unsuccessful.

If you don't provide any features, LightFM uses an identity matrix. This means that every user and every item get a single features that is unique to them.

If you do supply features, LightFM just takes them as given. This may mean a reduction in accuracy if your features are less expressive. If you stack things, you should do it column-wise: your output will be of shape `(num_items, num_items + num_metadata_features)`.

This is currently under-documented and is a frequent point of confusion. I will try to expand the docs soon.

Sure, you could do that. To construct the features matrix, you need to treat the views of one user over time as new users, giving the features new values where they have changed, and keeping them constant where they haven't. For example, if you had 100 users, and wanted 5 time-views of each, you'd end up with a feature matrix with 500 rows.
LightFM doesn't take ratings: it's an implicit feedback model (which is what you want; models with ratings is something you almost never want).

The problem is now Python 3.3 and 3.4 - since they don't have the interfaces, and since this code needs to be embedded in a .pth line, it's not really feasible to backport importlib machinery.

I think it's also not viable to include the behavior in a library (like setuptools) -- because this logic runs at startup, it probably shouldn't have dependencies on any functionality not in the stdlib. `pkg_dirs` is a list parameter, so it would be `conda config --add pkgs_dirs $HOME/.conda_packages`.  Doing that will probably completely replace the default value, so you'll want to run `conda config --show` first, see what the default values are, and make sure they end up exactly what you want after you do the `conda config --add pkgs_dirs $HOME/.conda_packages`.
Same here: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3756, just stumbled upon your issue...

Actually, I think this would be solved by something we're kicking around. The idea is to differentiate between *requested* packages and those installed *solely* as dependencies. We'd then allow `conda` to remove those "dependency" packages if necessary to accomplish upgrade goals. Of course, we'd always minimize the number of such removals, but in a case like this, it seems like it would be worthwhile.
@meawoppl `conda update conda` should fix this (or `conda update -f conda` if that gives the same error).

The `conda create --clone` command is close, but it clones an whole environment.  There's currently not a way just to pull particular packages out of an existing environment.

If it weren't for conda's support of pre-link and post-link scripts, you could take an environment and (except of the `info/` directory) perfectly recreate the conda packages that were linked into that environment.  Unfortunately pre-link and post-link preclude that ability.

Everyone, this is a big thread, and I don't have the time to read the whole thing, but this is territory we've been over before.  Below are some references.

The challenge is that it is very easy to get into a situation where there are no degrees of freedom.  There are many ways this can happen, and once it does if conda insists on keeping all packages with satisfied dependencies then there is nothing to do but to start uninstalling to break the log jam.

Here are some other issues that touch on this:

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/337 - conda doesn't just try to satisfy deps, it also tries to get the latest version of all deps that satisfy reqs, even if that means updating an otherwise perfectly functional set of pkgs.

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1696 - no distinction between "must have" and "nice to have" packages, so dep list can grow to be longer than is necessary

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/454 - check satisfiability of all env pkg deps at install time

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1268 - check satisfiability of all env pkg deps after install, and warn.

My preference would be to have a configurable `strict` setting, also available on the CLI, that would ensure that all packages at all times have their deps satisfied.  But if it is not set, then the unit of atomic pkg consistency is a single command.  Previous commands are not considered.

> The problem that is, even when the root environment is "read only" the /path/to/root/environment/envs directory is still on the "envs directories" list.

> Should /shared/anaconda3/5.0.0.1/envs be in the envs directories list?

Yes.  By default, there are two locations in the envs directories list.  One associated with the root environment and one associated with the current user's home environment.  The root environment is preferred if it's writable, otherwise we assume that the user's home environment is writable.  Just like the package cache, the requirement is that at least one of these directories is writable, for the creation of new environments.

However, read-only locations are still in the list.  Read-only environments can still be activated.  They just can't be installed into.

I'm not familiar with Pandas but presuming it takes a Python dictionary you should be able to convert a PyJMap to a normal Python dictionary.  PyJMap implements the Python mapping protocol so it appears as a dictionary to most commands but it's still a Java object that is wrapped inside a Python object.  In Java terms, PyJMap can be considered an interface that matches dictionary, but it doesn't support all the operations/functions a dictionary does.  So you could try looping over the map and turning it into a dictionary such as

```python
d = {}
for key in MyData:
    d[key] = MyData[key]
```

I recommend against the use of pbr for version management, for a couple reasons.

First, it's a new dependency. Each additional dependency makes Pulp Smash harder to learn, buggier, and harder to package.

Second, it significantly complicates the packaging machinery. It adds a layer on top of the well understood setuptools packaging machinery, while still requiring that the user understand how the underlying packaging machinery would work without pbr.

Third, it implements several misfeatures. For example: it [requires `requirements.txt`](https://docs.openstack.org/pbr/latest/user/features.html#requirements) and optionally `requirements-py3.txt` to specify dependencies. This is a **bad idea**. We should **not** be shipping a `requirements.txt` file with Pulp Smash. See: [setup.py vs requirements.txt](https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/). It also auto-generates an [overly-generous MANIFEST.in](https://docs.openstack.org/pbr/latest/user/features.html#manifest) file. It also auto-generates a changelog from git, which is easy to do with a simple script if desired, and not something that's desirable if one is writing good quality release notes.

Fourth, it significantly complicates things. Have I mentioned this yet? I should mention this again. It overrides the `build_sphinx` command. It requires the use of git and, uh, `Sem-Ver` pseudo-headers, whatever those are. It adds a `setup.cfg` file that's less flexible than `setup.py`, and just... contains the same information.

If we're going to put effort into complicating the packaging machinery, I'd suggest looking into something like [pipenv](https://docs.pipenv.org/) instead. It has a clear value proposition and is made by the well-respected Kenneth Reitz.

> A follow-up on that question would then be: Can I stop the interpreter from deleting the object when the interpreter goes out of scope? If not, or if it requires too much hacking, I'd be okay with a copy.

No.  When the interpreter goes out of scope there won't actually be a Python object left and so there would be no way back into the object.  It *is* possible, however, to have your data survive a interpreter shutdown and restart by having numpy operate on your own memory: pass a pointer to allocated memory (of at least the required size) into the `py::array`/`py::array_t` constructors.  Then then numpy object won't allocate anything itself, but will just directly access/modify your memory.

> I think we should at least explicitly delete the operator bool then, with a deprecation warning to use hasattr or contains instead (assuming a deprecation warning on a deleted method generates a useful compiler warning).

Sounds good.

> I'd also like to propose, if deprecating or deleting operator bool, adding a hasattr method to object_api: right now it feels quite inconsistent to have everything (including contains) as a method except for this one being a function. Keeping the function (for a python-like interface) is fine, but requiring the python-like interface here for something that naturally feels like it ought to be a method seems a little counterintuitive.

I'm not sure about duplicating `hasattr` as both a member and a free function. There's no strong advantage to the member function, so it's essentially a style choice -- one which diverges from Python's established syntax. If anything, I'd prefer to see `str`, `repr`, etc., as free functions and have `object_api` be mostly operators. `contains` is a bit unfortunate, since we can't replicate the `42 in mylist` syntax*, so the `__contains__` member becomes the next closest equivalent.

(* It is possible to make a custom infix operator in C++, so we could have a syntax like `42 <in> mylist` but that may be going too far with operator overloading.)

This page explain why the answer isn't exactly the same:

https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/806-3568/ncg_goldberg.html

In short doing a+(b+c) don't give the same result at (a+b)+c in float.
Doing the reduction in parallel change the order of addition, so it change
the result. But in this case, it should give a result that is more precise!
For the same inputs, compare the reduction in numpy in float32 vs in
float64 vs ne in float32 and float64. The ne result in float32 should be
closer to the float64 results then numpy in float32.

Note, this is true in general, I'm pretty sure there is corner case where
this is false, so to be really sure, it should be tried in multiple cases.

In Theano, we do the reduction with a float64 accumulator when the inputs
are in float32. This give more precise results without slowing down our
computation as the bottleneck is reading from the memory, not the
computation. I think numpy use a float32 accumulator, at least, in the past
that is what it was doing. I would suggest that you investigate for NE3 to
use a float64 accumulator by default, so by default NE3 would be faster and
more precisse then NE2 and NumPy.

Fred

Unfortunately the NumExpr 2.6 reductions are single threaded so you will
not see a performance improvement from using sum inside NumExpr. I would
suggest you try,

dist_2 = ne.evaluate('(nodes - node) ** 2').sum(axis=1)

I'm not clear on why for float32 there's a difference between NumPy and
NumExpr 2.6, but if it changes with doing the sumations as above with NumPy
after the return then likely the problem is within the NE reduction
machinery. Also try pre-allocation of your output to stop NumExpr from
using output buffering,

temp = np.empty_like( nodes )
ne.evaluate( '(nodes-node)**2', out=temp)
dist_2 = temp.sum(axis=1)

That will be harder on your RAM and memory bandwidth, however. Also try
transposing your matrix and averaging over axis 0 instead, since I assume
you have C-ordered arrays?

How much RAM do you have? Creating additional permanent arrays for
nodes_sqr in memory is of course simple (for both NumPy and NumExpr) but
your array is already 4 GB. It may help to pre-compute this but on the
other hand it may simply further restrict your memory bandwidth. It would
depend on a lot on your computer's configuration.

How many threads are you using for NumExpr? Don't use hyperthreading with
it, use the number of virtual cores.

It just occurred to me that this may not be very clear from our docs currently: if `consume()` raises an exception like that (which is an exception that's re-raised from a defunct worker thread), it will not recover from that. For non-negative `xmin` they are the same, but they can differ for negative `xmin`, e.g.

```
>>> xmin = -1
>>> np.floor(xmin + 0.5).astype(int)
-1

>>> int(xmin + 0.5)
0
```

I think there has been some misunderstanding here. When performing domain validation challenges required by the server, Certbot's Nginx plugin may need to set `server_names_hash_bucket_size` so it can configure Nginx to respond to abnormally long domain names. [The logic](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/68e37b03c821560e7f316d260f8da97ef3e2087c/certbot-nginx/certbot_nginx/tls_sni_01.py#L109) for determining if this value should be set is it looks in the `http` block of your main Nginx configuration file (`/etc/nginx/nginx.conf` unless you've explicitly set it to something else). If it finds an existing `server_names_hash_bucket_size` directive but it is set to a number smaller than 128, our plugin temporarily increases the value, otherwise, it ignores it. The Nginx plugin does not currently check if the value has been set in any included files.

Because of only checking the main nginx.conf file, the value you set in `/etc/nginx/conf.d/01-server-names.conf` isn't noticed, our plugin adds a duplicate directive, and Nginx becomes unhappy. Setting this value in your main Nginx configuration file should have the same effect as where you've defined it, so while our plugin should do better, you should be able to work around the problem by moving the directive there rather than the included file.

In an earlier post, you said:

> Even with server_names_hash_bucket_size 128; in my nginx.conf, certbot will crash nginx with the same error message.

If you've moved this directive to your main Nginx config file and `nginx -t` works but Certbot does not, please provide a copy of your main Nginx config file.

I hope this helps! I'm reopening this issue as I think there was a misunderstanding about the part of the plugin causing the problem here, but if I'm mistaken, please tell me I'm wrong and close the issue again.

@zjs, thanks for taking a look at this and I'm sorry I didn't respond sooner. I think we have a renewed reason to look into this with all of the DNS plugins. Do we want `certbot-auto` to include all of the DNS plugins by default? I'm happy to create a new issue for this, but the two problems are definitely related and I think it's worth discussing this now as it's much harder to change our decision after we've shipped with certain packages included.

I haven't yet looked at how large these new packages and their dependencies are. Some such as `boto3` for route-53 for AWS are quite large and was roughly 25% of our current venv when I looked a while ago. People run `certbot-auto` on all sorts of devices such as Raspberry Pis, systems with poor internet connections, etc. It might be worthwhile designing a system that allows users to easily specify what plugins they want installed. I think it'll be pretty rare for someone to need multiple DNS plugins on their system. The downside of doing this is it hurts discoverability as we discussed before.

I think the proposal above is pretty good, but there are two potential problems:

1. Users who place the script in something like `/usr/local/bin` might not want to be forced to create a directory at the same location to use other plugins.
2. We'd have to be pretty careful with the permissions on the plugin directories. Since `certbot-auto` uses `sudo` a lot internally and is often run from things like cron/systemd where it may already have root privileges, having sane permissions on these directories and files is very important.

For the purposes of brainstorming, alternatives to scripts could be just a list of python python packages or a pip requirements file. The downside of these approaches is it may not allow hash checking or installation of non-Python dependencies. We could also have users places these things at a predefined location, allow a flag for it to be configurable, or even have the user modify `certbot-auto` itself if it's something simple like a list of packages.

I'm not sure the best approach here but I wanted to reopen this conversation. In the end, it'd be good to get Erik's opinion as he's the author of `certbot-auto` and knows the most about Python packaging but I'm avoiding mentioning him now until we have something we like as I believe he's pretty busy right now.

@pombredanne The downside of the metapackage (which is very clever, btw) is you're trusting a whole mess of unverified downloads from PyPI to do the right thing as root on your box. certbot-auto contains a requirements file with sha-256 hashes of all the PyPI downloads and checks them as it downloads. If you're going to install using pip, I recommend extracting [those files](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces) and pointing pip to them: `pip install -r ...`. Obviously, it's a trust-on-first-use arrangement, but it's better than having faith in the certificate authority system, which has a zillion entities with do-anything access.

A prime example is widgetsnbextension from ipywidgets, which, ironically, is an "official" jupyter notebook extension. widgetsnbextension requires both JavaScript and Python module to work, but if your notebook server loads the JavaScript for widgetsnbextension version 2.0.0 while your kernel has the widgetsnbextension version 3.0.0 Python package, it won't work: the notebook would complain about incompatibility.

But why should I expect that every kernel has the same widgetsnbextension version 2.0.0 Python package? It's entirely possible that different notebooks depend on different kernels, which are based on different virtualenvs, which have different versions of the widgetsnbextension Python package.

If I understand correctly, the current mechanism installs and enables extensions either per-user (`--user`) or per-virtualenv (`--sys-prefix`). But even in the per-virtualenv case, extension JavaScript is loaded from the virtualenv that is used to run the notebook _server_ (this includes `jupyter notebook` and `jupyterhub-singleuser` instances spawned by JupyterHub). Extension JavaScript is not loaded from the virtualenv of the notebook's kernel. This mechanism seems really broken to me.

The process is running the IPython kernel - it waits for a message such as an execution request, then processes it and goes back to waiting for another message.

When you fork, there are now two processes running the same code. The parent process behaves normally, but the child process finishes the code you wrote and goes back to waiting for a message on sockets that belong to the parent process. I don't know exactly what's meant to happen in that case, but I'm pretty sure it's not supported.

Forking is fiddly. If you do it in the kernel, I'd guess you need to make absolutely sure that the child process exits when it finishes the code you meant it to run, e.g. by calling [os._exit(0)](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os._exit).

  }
}
```

When user slides slider `r`, we emit a Redux action like:

```js
{
  type: "UPDATE_USER_MODEL",
  modelID: 'my-image-model',
  value: "200",
  path: ["r"]
}
```

Within reducers, modify `['models', modelID, "r"]`, updating the value for `r` in the model while also sending a `COMM_MESSAGE` to the backend to also update the model in the kernel.

The kernel responds by sending a `COMM_MESSAGE` with `{ "path": ["r"], value: "200" }`
and then also sends the a `COMM_MESSAGE` with a payload for the modified image:

```js
{ "path": ["image"], value: "somebinarydata" }
```

I've had this same need (to execute vacuum). My solution was to just manually implement the execution and output myself (below).

```python
def run(self):
       connection = psycopg2.connect('dbname={db} host={host} port={port} user={user} password={pass}'.format(args_dict))
        cursor = connection.cursor()

        # change isolation level temporarily to allow vacuum
        old_isolation_level = connection.isolation_level
        connection.set_isolation_level(0)
        cursor.execute(self.query)   # self.query is just 'vacuum;'
        connection.commit()
        connection.set_isolation_level(old_isolation_level)

        # output - update market table
        connection_target = self.output().connect()
        self.output().touch(connection_target)
        connection_target.commit()
        connection_target.close()
```

see [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1017463/postgresql-how-to-run-vacuum-from-code-outside-transaction-block) and [here](http://initd.org/psycopg/docs/connection.html#connection.autocommit) for two psycopg2 solutions to execute a vacuum.

>In the interest of being able to insert and update large datasets because of API call limits, I use db.bulk_docs()

FWIW each document in a `_bulk_docs` write will count towards the API write limits (I think that was omitted initially) so you'll ultimately need a plan with an appropriate number of writes anyway. That said bulk transfers are more efficient - so you're doing the correct thing to use bulk for mass inserts. 

However, there is a HTTP request size limit on the server, which I believe is currently 1 MB. Given your 2 MB data it seems more likely that size limit is the cause of your `Connection aborted`, rather than a timeout. The server does return a `413` before aborting the connection, but we have noticed some HTTP libraries don't read data from the server until they've finished sending and so fail with broken connections instead of the correct error code. It [looks like requests](https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/2422#issuecomment-258370446) is another one of these libraries.

I would imagine hue only fetches enough of your data to display and would be much slower if you tried to pull back the full result set. Python is never going to be the fastest way to pull back large amounts of data. For bulk exports of data your best bet is to write the table out as text and read the actual data from hdfs. If you want to save bandwidth you could also write out the result set compressed and pull that back. LZO is good for this as its not particularly taxing for cpu on the client side so the bottleneck is usually bandwidth when fetching the data.

If i set  convert_types=False on the cursor, testing on a table with a million rows with a couple timestamp and decimal columns my run time goes from 90 seconds to 15. This is understandable as profiling the codes shows loads of time spent in decimal and strptime functions. However, strangely enough when this is disabled my cursor is still returning decimals and datetime objects perfectly fine.

@wesm is this taken care of upstream now so the option should be removed from impyla altogether?

Additionally, there are some other ways of speeding things up and not using so much resource.

If you don't need all the data in memory at once and you are doing some other operation that is more likely to be the bottleneck it makes sense to use the cursor as an iterator which you can loop over rather than wait for the whole result set to be pulled back.

```python
sql_query='select * from some_db.some_table'
imp_cur.execute(sql_query)
for row in imp_cur:
    do_someting    
```

There is also the fetchcolumnar method which returns batches of rows. This is about 2.5 times as fast for me but you have to pull the full resultset back as there doesn't currently seem to be a builtin way to  iterate over batches as they are received.

```python
sql_query='select * from dome_db.some_table'
imp_cur=get_cursor('impala')
imp_cur.execute(sql_query)
batches=imp_cur.fetchcolumnar()
for batch in batches:
    dosometing
````

@seanzxx I updated my PR with the apple suggested method of closing anchors with -X token
You get the token by running ```sudo pfctl -a com.apple/sshuttle -s Reference```
```
PID      Process Name                 TOKEN                    TIMESTAMP
5191     pfctl                        10051342025653898869     0 days 00:00:07
```
when you close sshuttle you can see that the correct token is used to close this anchor.
```
>> pfctl -X 10051342025653898869
```
If the token is not found then we use this method to close. 
```
pfctl('-dF', 'all')
```
This way systems using pfctl that are not OSX still close and flush rules.

As I see, little bug in Django `make_model_tuple` func may be cause of this problem:

```
from django.utils import six


def make_model_tuple(model):
    """
    Takes a model or a string of the form "app_label.ModelName" and returns a
    corresponding ("app_label", "modelname") tuple. If a tuple is passed in,
    it's assumed to be a valid model tuple already and returned unchanged.
    """
    try:
        if isinstance(model, tuple):
            model_tuple = model
        elif isinstance(model, six.string_types):
            app_label, model_name = model.split(".")
            model_tuple = app_label, model_name.lower()
        else:
            model_tuple = model._meta.app_label, model._meta.model_name
        assert len(model_tuple) == 2
        return model_tuple
    except (ValueError, AssertionError):
        raise ValueError(
            "Invalid model reference '%s'. String model references "
            "must be of the form 'app_label.ModelName'." % model
        )

```

If I do `source ~.cache/LHCbDIRAC_ENV/x86_64-slc6-gcc49-opt/v8r2p27` at the command line I get a whole host of errors:
```
bash: 45: command not found
bash: 31m: command not found
bash: 31m: command not found
bash: 5: command not found
bash: 27:ln=38: command not found
bash: 5: command not found
bash: 51:mh=44: command not found
bash: 38: command not found
bash: 5: command not found
bash: 15:pi=40: command not found
bash: 38: command not found
...
```
This is due to lines like:
```bash
GREP_COLOR=37;45
LESS=-F -g -i -M -R -S -w -X -z-4
```
which are not sourceable bash commands. If they were quoted like:
```bash
GREP_COLOR="37;45"
LESS="-F -g -i -M -R -S -w -X -z-4"
```

The IEEE EUI-64 identifier is an administered identifier typically configured in the factory.  While factory-assigned identifiers are typically assumed to be globally unique, there are a number of instances where that assumption was violated (flaws in manufacturing, counterfeit devices, etc.).

In the context of Thread, devices using the same IEEE Extended Address in the same network leads to the following issues:
1. IEEE 802.15.4 MAC security uses Extended Addresses in the Nonce and relies on having unique Extended Addresses so that a give Nonce value is used at most once for a given AES-128 key.
2. MLE uses Extended Addresses to uniquely identify neighboring devices.  If two devices attempt to attach with the same Extended Address, messages will be dropped due to conflicting frame counters.

For these reasons, the broader industry (IETF, Thread Group, etc.) are migrating away from using factory-assigned unique identifiers towards stable but randomly-chosen identifiers.  From past industry experience, the likelihood of two devices randomly selecting the same identifier is much less likely than the occurrence of duplicates in factory-assigned identifiers.

Regarding the use case for `otLinkSetExtendedAddress()`, note that IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4 communication may occur before Thread protocols are enabled via `otThreadSetEnabled()`.  Nest uses this feature to support some legacy protocols.

Speaking for myself, there are a few reasons that I find fault with this PR;

1) The otPlatRadio API is intended to be an interface for Openthread. It is not intended to be used by anything else and should not be forced to accommodate anything else.
2) Dynamic callbacks presume a run-time change is supported by the module when in fact it is not.  If you were to change these callbacks while OT was operating then that could result in unexpected race conditions for which there are currently no protections.  Even if you are using it in a way that avoids these race conditions that doesn't mean someone else won't come along and see that this is possible and then use it in a different / unsupported way.
3) Yes the pointer memory is in fact a concern. As well as the extra code space required to de-reference the pointers to make the function calls.  I have been RAM and flash constrained far too often to not cringe when I see unnecessary consumption of those resources.
4) There is a desire to support OT running on a host.  The solution we have been discussing involves optionally moving Spinel below the otPlatRadio API such that calls from OT to otPlatRadio API's would result in one or more Spinel commands to a as yet undefined interface on the NPC.  This PR does not get us to that goal, but if that solution already existed then I believe your needs would be satisfied as well making this PR unnecessary.

> just do `apt-get install libmemcached-dev`

Well, that would work around the issue for this particular install, but that also means that any project in which I wish to use pytest-services will also have that prerequisite step, which is a substantial burden, especially when these projects aren't even using memcached at all. So while my tests would pass here, when this next release of pytest-services is cut, it will break in every environment in which it's used unless those environments are prepared in advance.

> why be so radical?

A pytest plugin should in general not add unnecessary dependencies and should be particularly careful not to add prerequisites (like having a C compiler, having memcached sources), as these prerequisites are very expensive to handle in the general case. In other words, with the exception of plugins specifically designed to work in a particular environment, plugins should be installable by Python and Pip without additional requirements.

I'm open to a few options: 

- Make pylibmc an `extra` dependency. Projects that wish to pull in pylibmc with pytest-services can do so with `pytest-services[memcache]`.
- Make pylibmc an implicit dependency. Projects that wish to have memcached support must install pylibmc in their project. Any tests requiring the client fixture will be skipped if it's not present.
- Move the memcached service plugin to another package.

>
>    - DateTimeType gets time zone support plus a built-in parser.

> No external dependencies are needed. The only open question is what

> PR #310 <https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/310> added support
> for a UTC designator in the parsing format but did nothing to actually
> handle the time zone information. Going forward, there are several
> reasonable options:
>
>    -
>
>    (A) Accept all kinds of inputs and attach tzinfo only if the input
>    contains time zone information.
>    - 2015-01-01 12:00:00-07:00 would be imported as datetime(2015, 1, 1,
>       12, 0, tzinfo=offset_timezone(-07:00))
>       - Pros: Has the least surprising to_native outcome.
>       - Cons: Resulting objects may be either naive or aware depending on
>       input.
>    -
>
>    (B) Accept all kinds of inputs. Convert timezone-aware values to UTC
>    and drop the tzinfo objects.
>    - 2015-01-01 12:00:00-07:00 would be imported as datetime.datetime(2015,
>       1, 1, 19, 0)
>       - Pros: An optimized setup in terms of actual application design.
>       Compatible; preserves current behavior for both presently accepted formats.
>       - Cons: Opinionated. Not viable for fields that must be able to
>       accept both local times and timezone-aware values, but I believe this to be
>       rare.
>    -
>
>    (C) Revert #310 <https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/310>
>    and stop accepting values with timezones by default.
>    - 2015-01-01 12:00:00-07:00 would be rejected.
>       - Pros: Having to explicitly enable time zone support means that
>       there will be no surprises in the field's behavior.
>       - Cons: Kinda 1988.
>
> I don't have a definite favorite, but I'm leaning towards B because it
> represents a kind of best practice. In most cases it's ideal to work in UTC
> internally; in addition, dropping the redundant tzinfo components helps
> prevent bugs. While this setup would keep the current conflation between
> <datetime> and <datetime>Z, it's probably not a big deal, since local
> datetimes mainly appear in user-facing apps and aware datetimes in APIs.

@nealwu We had verified that all emails signed the CLA. Once we got to the "so there's good news and bad news" state, we _couldn't_ merge, because Googlebot failed to report a status, so the required check was blocking. Usually it gives that message and approves the check; sometimes it just fails.

We're wondering what you do at tensorflow/tensorflow when you run into the same googlebot bug.

> > unless we can assume contiguous steps, the only way to index into the "reservoir" at index i is to scan through [...]
> 
> For image summaries, the row data in Tensors is tiny. In theory those rows should be a contiguous block of pointers to TensorStrings. Are we only scanning through that tiny subsection of Tensors? Or are these queries scanning the entire Tensors table?

So this is all true if reservoir sampling is enabled with a small reservoir size, but without it the rows aren't necessarily contiguous and there could be many rows per image summary.

In the unsampled case, we can use TensorSeriesStepIndex to filter down Tensors to just those rows that are for an image tag (just pushed a commit fixing this, that index is tricky because you need to filter out null steps in order to use it), but even if the data per row is tiny it's still a lot of extra rows to scan.  E.g. if someone has 100 image summary tags and 10,000 images per tag, then we still have to scan the full 10,000 rows for each tag and it ends up being a scan of 1 million rows.  In contrast, if the sample count were represented at the Tag level, we wouldn't need to hit the Tensors table at all, so it would only be 100 indexed lookups into Tags/Runs.

> > we end up needing to scan over every single image summary entry in Tensors. This is because index_impl() needs to know the sample count for each image tag
> 
> We could can add an optional count field to the Tags table definition. Then the image plugin asks the writer to enable counting for its tags. This shouldn't be difficult since our design assumes a single writer. But would it be necessary? If the count just scans over the tiny contiguous pointer rows, maybe that's ok?

See above re: why I think it is worth fixing this.  We could introduce a sample count column to the Tags table, but it's sort of privileging the specific concept of multiply-sampled summaries and I wanted to just get a better sense of how dedicated we are to this concept vs just implementing it in terms of multiple related tags like tf.summary.image() does.  I don't know the full history, but from #315 and #296 it sounded like the main motivation of bundling multiple image samples into one tensor was primarily for convenience in mapping it to a single tf.summary.tensor_summary() call - but as I described above there seem like other ways we could handle that concern.

Another motivation given was flexibility, but I actually think we get a bit more flexibility and power out of the notion of "relationships between tags" than we do out of "tags with multiple samples within a tensor value".  In particular, with a "relationships between tags" concept, we open up the ability to do stuff like have a text summary tag "attached" to an image summary tag (e.g. for image captioning) or a histogram/bar chart summary tag "attached" to an image summary tag (e.g. for showing classification probabilities).  I think that could be really cool, and that concept would easily include a set of "attached" image summaries representing samples, at which point we don't need to special case that behavior.

> > The main other issue is that the individual image URLs identify images not by their step (which is unchanging) but by their index within the reservoir
> 
> Could it be addressed by bolting on some query parameters to the http endpoint?

Perhaps - I actually did mention that we'll probably want to replace the index query parameter with the step count - but using the step count instead introduces the other issue of what we do when that step has been purged in a reservoir sampling case.  I'd suggested using the next higher step, but now I'm actually thinking it may be better to return some error message that renders the image as a blank, with some superimposed text saying e.g. "reload page" - that way we aren't misleading users by showing an image from step Y when they thought they were going to step X.

    port 80 https

        template client-ssl X
```

The same problem breaks opensmtpd:
```
kousu@comms:~$ sudo /usr/sbin/smtpd -d
warn:  /etc/letsencrypt/live/comms.kousu.ca/privkey.pem: insecure permissions: must be at most rwx------ 
fatal: load_pki_tree: failed to load key file
```

There's something else strange here: the symlink's permissions are 777, even though the actual file isn't. OpenSMTPd is checking permissions on the symlink, not the actual file. The permissions of the symlinks should match the permissions of the files!

But that doesn't solve this problem in general. Some programs are going to be more or less picky about permissions than others, and I suspect some will flat out contradict (e.g. if a program assumes the certs are owned by its user, or difference of opinion about if certs should be read pre or post priv-dropping). #1504 argues that each specific piece of software should get a different adaptor script. I don't know what's best.

Incidentally, changing the symlink to be *more* restrictive than the actual file fixes OpenSMTPd:
```
root@comms:/etc/letsencrypt/live/comms.kousu.ca# chmod go-rwx privkey.pem 
root@comms:/etc/letsencrypt/live/comms.kousu.ca# smtpd -d
info: OpenSMTPD 5.4.2p1 starting
info: startup
```

Thanks for the PR @xgin. I know these are the changes proposed by @pde but I personally do not like this approach. The reason is that this changes the plugin API and will break plugins that don't have a `is_root_required` property (there is no requirement that they inherit from `letsencrypt.plugins.common.Plugin`).

I'm still in favor of the solution I discussed in IRC of simply removing the warning. To post my reasoning here, the "Root (sudo) is required to run most of letsencrypt functionality" message is only a warning. It does not stop execution so plugins still have to handle the case where they have insufficient permissions. Removing the warning stops suggesting people run the client with root privileges when it may not be necessary (such as when using `manual`, `webroot`, or `plesk` plugins). This approach also goes with the idea that it's easier to ask for forgiveness than permission.

If we did this, I think we should make sure sane error messages are thrown when we have insufficient permissions, but I suspect this will be a pretty straight forward fix.

If pde wants to use this approach, he's certainly able to merge this PR, but I personally think we can do better.

Not at the moment.  I could make up an example but it wouldn't be much use, because it would only cover a tiny subset of the malformations possible in real data and would have at most a dozen or so lines rather than millions, so would seem to be a trivial problem easily fixed by hand.

I've already mentioned two of the more common problems (commas in text fields, unmatched quotes).  Others include commas in numeric fields (e.g. "1,048,576" or locales that use commas as the decimal separator), carriage returns and/or line feeds in unquoted text fields, and more.  It's difficult to remember or even think of all the weird data problems I've encountered over the decades that i've been doing this kind of data mangling - every data file or data source is different, and you need to be adaptable, and have flexible tools, to deal with them.

As a rule of thumb, the easiest and most time, RAM, & CPU efficient method is to first search the input data for a character that **doesn't** appear anywhere in the input and then use that as the output delimiter - if your data is full of commas, spaces and tabs, use colons.  If it has colons, use semi-colons or commas or tabs.  or equals, or underscore. or anything that doesn't appear in the input.  Sometimes you have to choose something that only appears rarely in the input, first transforming it to something else.

spaces in space-delimited text fields are also extremely common, but not all that relevant to csvkit's output (but can cause problems for csvkit's input processing).

These issues are often the reason **why** you're processing the file in the first place - to clean it up and normalise it because it's full of horrors like this and someone, perhaps you, needs to import it into some other program or a database or just analyse it and produce a summary.

The list under [compatible-devices](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense#compatible-devices) was suggesting minimal required firmware versions. Since librealsense is not actually enforcing these limitations the list was removed for now. 
Until stated otherwise, librealsense remains an experimental and community project, meaning we can't physically test all possible combinations of device, firmware, OS, kernel version, USB controller etc.. 
We will make effort to enable any official Intel firmware, but each RealSense product is being distributed with a particular support matrix in mind. For example, while there is nothing stopping you from working with SR300 3.10.10 using librealsense, the only configuration that is officially supported by Intel for the SR300 is RealSense SDK on Windows 10 with the latest firmware as published through Windows Update.

@tufeixp the `camera_options` enum is used for both sensors and processing blocks (such as the colorizer and filters). The supported options for a sensor are not all possible `camera_options` so you only see part of them. To enable holes filling you need to apply the Spatial Filter on the image before creating a texture from it (and set its option of Holes Filling to 5). This is not support yet by the Unity wrapper and was actually only added yesterday to the C# wrapper (PR #1169, thanks to @satoshi-maemoto).
We are planning on enriching the Unity integration with additional features, and creating a 3D mesh is a desired one, unfortunately at the moment I don't know when this will be ready, but as we've already said we try to be open with our work and will share this information once it is concrete.

@Fedalto: Thanks for the feedback. We really need discussion, since this is complicated.

First, just to clarify: The CamelCase conversion is done to the enum *type* name, the UPPERCASE conversion happens to the enum *key* names. Graphene converts method names, but not type names, because Python has already the right name convention for these. However, databases usually have lower case type names for their enum types. Like in [this example](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/10/static/datatype-enum.html):

    CREATE TYPE user_mood AS ENUM ('sad', 'ok', 'happy');

In SQLAlchemy you would write this type as

    sqlalchemy.Enum('sad', 'ok', 'happy', name='user_mood')

It makes sense to convert this to a Graphene GraphQL Enum Type with he name `UserMood` and keys `SAD`, `OK` and `HAPPY` because that's what you expect in GraphQL, and on the other hand you don't want to have CamelCase names in the database. Graphene is not in the business of converting database types, so I don't think we can speak of an inconsistency here.

The complexity comes from the fact that we are dealing with 4 different kinds of enums: The actual database enum type, the SQLAlchemy Enum type that reflects it, the Python Enum that might (or might not) be underlying that SQLAlchemy Enum, and finally the graphene Enum GraphQL type.

Note that the SQLAlchemy Enum type must not necessarily be based off a Python Enum. You can define a SQLAlchemy Enum simply like in the example above, without using a Python Enum, or you can use a Python Enum like this:

    PyUserMoodType = enum.Enum('UserMood', 'sad ok happy')
    SaUserMoodType = sqlalchemy.Enum(PyUserMoodType)

In this case, the type name would be converted to `usermood` on the database.

My point is, when using graphene_sqlalchemy, we must use the SQLAlchemy Enum type as the source for converting to GraphQL, not the Python Enum type, because we might not even have one and because graphene_sqlalchemy operates one level higher in the abstraction.

The typical example is, like in your case, you have a property `user_mood` in an SQLAlchemy base class `User` like this:

    UserMood = sqlalchemy.Enum('sad', 'ok', 'happy', name='user_mood')

    class User(Base):
        __tablename__ = 'users'

        id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
        user_name = Column(String)
        user_mood = Column(UserMood)

    class UserType(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
        class Meta:
            model = User

The question is how we can use the automatically created graphene enum type for the user mood in Arguments (like a filter in a query or in a mutation). We can't just redefine it with `graphene.Enum` because, as you noticed we then would have two different enum types. My solution was using the registry - that already exists in graphene_sqlalchemy - to not only store the types for sqlalchemy object types, but also for sqlalchemy enums. So we could do this:

    from graphene_sqlalchemy.registry import registry

    users = List(UserType, kind=Argument(registry.get_type_for_enum(UserMood)))

This would use the exact same GraphQL type for the argument as it is used on the column.

Alternatively, if you want to avoid using the registry, you can also do this:

    users = List(UserType, kind=Argument(UserType._meta.fields['pet_kind'].type))

I know this is a bit ugly (particularly because it is accessing a "private" attribute), but still readable. The expression simply gets the (GraphQL) type of the 'pet_kind' field of the UserType GraphQL type.

Sorry for this lengthy explanation, but as I said this matter *is* complicated because of all the layers of abstraction and "impedence mismatch" on all the layers.

> Improve exception message when the type of fromlist is unicode. fromlist
parameter of `__import__()` only accepts str in Python 2 and this will help to
identify the problem especially when the unicode_literals future import is
used.

It's not clear to me why this code should support packages that are in violation of the language spec. This issue is going to be lurking for those packages when used elsewhere.

That said, the changes to the code are fairly innocuous, so I'd only be a -0 on this personally. @tseaver may have a different opinion (I know he considers `unicode_literals` to be a code smell, and I've come to agree).

Discussed this with @vidartf and @SylvainCorlay, and this is gonna stay as it is now, although I still not like it as it is.

My issue with this is (after renaming VideoStream to Video) that `Video` inherits from `MediaStream`, but in the browser API, a `VideoHTMLElement` is not a `MediaStream`, but has a captureStream method that would result in a MediaStream. 
To model this (if you want to stay close to the browser API) you could create a `MediaStream` widget, and to signal that a widget has this interface, you could have a `HasMediaStream` interface on both the kernel and frontend side. 
We couldn't agree on the details, and I don't think @vidartf saw the point of it, so we decided to keep it as it is for now, and let it evolve in the ipywebrtc repo.

So I think we can close this for now, unless there are strong reasons to merge this.

* [`Non-Matching Headers`](https://github.com/roll/goodtables-next/blob/master/goodtables/spec.json#L83) and [`Badly Ordered Headers`](https://github.com/roll/goodtables-next/blob/master/goodtables/spec.json#L93) should require [`Missing Headers`](https://github.com/roll/goodtables-next/blob/master/goodtables/spec.json#L73)

the reason it is currently latin-1 (which in hindsight was probably not the best workaround) is because although RFC 4880 stipulates UTF-8, it has not always been this way:
- [RFC 1991](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1991#section-6.7) stipulated that the contents should be printable ASCII characters
- [RFC 2440](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2440#section-5.11) allowed for non-text values

I have encountered some UserIDs in the wild that are likely legacy ones that have been carried forward, which use extended ASCII characters like \xe3 and \xe9 that are not valid utf-8 characters (and do not decode correctly with 'ascii' but do with 'latin-1'). **TL;DR: It's time to use the iterator with a reasonable default limit.**

The idea that we're making clear where the API calls are doesn't actually fix the problem. If you give me back chunks and page tokens, I'll write a loop and then we're back where we started. If I forget my exit condition, we're in the same boat I was before, except you made me write more code to do it:

```python
token = True
api_call_count = 0
while token is not None and api_call_count < 10:
  topics, token = client.list_topics(next_page_token=token)
  api_call_count += 1
  for topic in topics:
    do_something_with_topic(topic)
```

This sucks for two reasons:

1. I don't really care about the number of API calls here (they cost $0.0000004 each), I care about the number of topics I get back to make my app work.
1. There's no guarantee I'll get N items per page, so depending on how PubSub feels today, I'll get a different number of topics as a result.

The right answer for me is being able to say "don't give me more than N topics", and understanding that this could mean a varying number of API calls:

```python
token = True
topic_count = 0
while token is not None and topic_count < 500:
  topics, token = client.list_topics(next_page_token=token)
  for topic in topics:
    topic_count += 1
    do_something_with_topic(topic)
```

That seems more reasonable to me... however I'm still writing more code, and I still have to remember the exit condition. As a matter of fact, it's more difficult for me to do what I want and just as easy for me to mess up and loop forever over billions of topics. There's no way to get multiple pages with this model *without* writing my own loop and putting in a manual exit condition.

In other words, if the problem we're trying to solve is "don't make people stupidly loop over everything", the right answer is likely:

```python
class Client:
  def list_topics(self, ..., limit=1000):
    return Iterator(...)

for topic in client.list_topics():
  do_something_with(topic)
```

This means the "loop forever" call would be...

```python
for topic in client.list_topics(limit=None):
  do_something_with(topic)
```

IIRC, we had the same discussion before with listing the objects in a bucket, and it currently returns an [iterator of sorts](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/cefff49fb57eea68e15e3a64f1a71c1ca107f44d/gcloud/storage/bucket.py#L245).

I believe that we had decided to have [a reasonable default](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/cefff49fb57eea68e15e3a64f1a71c1ca107f44d/gcloud/storage/bucket.py#L84) on how much listing we'd do, however it seems we only implemented that for the recursive delete and the global `make_public`

Regardless of all of this, based on our pricing, I could understand the argument for Datastore however that goes out the window due to Datastore's `LIMIT` operator (aka, you're expected to say how many results you want back). For PubSub, if you created 1m topics, and looped over them, it'd take hours and cost you a whole 40 cents.

I think it's unreasonable to leak this paging abstraction which makes the API obnoxious to use simply to avoid someone spending $0.40. If you want the library to show you exactly where your API calls are made, you should be using https://github.com/google/google-api-python-client which is 1:1 with the API...

``Datatable.Meta.columns`` will let you name columns you want to appear without declaring the whole thing from scratch.  The whole of ``Datatable.Meta`` is basically the old ``View.datatable_options`` dict, except you can give names in ``columns`` that refer to virtual fields declared on the class.

In most cases, you won't need to redeclare the column from scratch unless you intend to build a compound column or use a non-default Column type.
